critic_raw:
The SKS model uses multi-task learning with sentiment analysis as an auxiliary task to improve hate speech detection. The core methodological assumption is that sentiment information is reliably predictive of hate speech in a way that improves upon hate speech signal alone. This assumption is empirically testable but not guaranteed: sentiment and hate speech are correlated but not aligned — hateful text can be expressed in neutral or even positive sentiment framings (e.g., sarcastic or ironic hate), while strongly negative sentiment text is frequently not hateful. If the training datasets have a spurious correlation between negative sentiment and hate speech labels (which is plausible in many benchmark datasets), the multi-task setup would reinforce this spurious correlation rather than learning genuine hate speech signal.

Category embeddings from a dictionary of derogatory words introduce a vocabulary-dependent bias: the model's performance is bounded by the coverage of the derogatory word dictionary. Slurs and derogatory terms evolve rapidly, especially in online contexts, and a static dictionary will be incomplete for any test set collected after the dictionary was assembled. If the test datasets share temporal context with the dictionary assembly, the evaluation overestimates the method's robustness to novel hate speech vocabulary.

The multi-head attention architecture for SKS introduces a hyperparameter (number of attention heads) that controls how sentiment and hate speech representations are weighted. If this hyperparameter was tuned on the validation set for each dataset, the multi-task improvement may reflect hyperparameter optimization rather than the inherent benefit of sentiment knowledge sharing. The reproducibility study needs to distinguish hyperparameter sensitivity from architectural benefit.

Evaluation on only two benchmark datasets is insufficient to support the claim that sentiment knowledge sharing 'enhances hate speech detection' generally. Benchmark hate speech datasets are known to be highly domain-specific (Twitter vs. forums vs. news comments) and have different base rates of hate speech, different class imbalances, and different annotation schemes. Performance on two datasets cannot establish that the correlation between sentiment and hate speech is a stable signal across contexts.
all_issues_raised: Sentiment and hate speech are correlated but not aligned — hateful text with neutral or ironic sentiment framing would be misclassified, and if benchmark datasets have a spurious negative-sentiment/hate correlation the multi-task setup reinforces that artifact rather than learning genuine hate speech signal.

Category embeddings from a static derogatory word dictionary are bounded by dictionary coverage; if the test set was collected in the same temporal window as the dictionary, the evaluation overestimates robustness to novel hate speech vocabulary.

The number of attention heads is a hyperparameter that controls sentiment-hate speech weighting; if it was tuned per-dataset on the validation set, observed multi-task improvements may reflect hyperparameter optimization rather than the benefit of sentiment knowledge sharing.

Two benchmark datasets are insufficient to establish that sentiment-hate speech correlation is a stable generalizable signal; benchmark hate speech datasets are highly domain-specific with different base rates, class imbalances, and annotation schemes.
all_issues_adjudicated: Sentiment and hate speech are correlated but not aligned — hateful text with neutral or ironic sentiment framing would be misclassified, and if benchmark datasets have a spurious negative-sentiment/hate correlation the multi-task setup reinforces that artifact rather than learning genuine hate speech signal.

Category embeddings from a static derogatory word dictionary are bounded by dictionary coverage; if the test set was collected in the same temporal window as the dictionary, the evaluation overestimates robustness to novel hate speech vocabulary.

The number of attention heads is a hyperparameter that controls sentiment-hate speech weighting; if it was tuned per-dataset on the validation set, observed multi-task improvements may reflect hyperparameter optimization rather than the benefit of sentiment knowledge sharing.

Two benchmark datasets are insufficient to establish that sentiment-hate speech correlation is a stable generalizable signal; benchmark hate speech datasets are highly domain-specific with different base rates, class imbalances, and annotation schemes.
